Wildfire loss estimates are influenced by drought and climate trends, population growth in high-hazard areas, and construction culture in wildfire-prone areas (Aon Benfield 2016; Kramer et al., 2019).

The rapid development of land in California has outpaced the enhancement of scientific measures to estimate the wildfire risk, evaluate building materials, and provide policy makers with methodical long-term views (Moore, 1981).

Structures built in the State Responsibility Area (SRA) and high and very high fire hazard severity zones in the Local Responsibility Areas (LRAs) must meet specific building materials, systems, and assembly requirements.
The hazard intensity from local fuels can vastly vary, which directly influences the performance of different building materials (Caton et al., 2017).
Also, as previously mentioned, the enforcement of Chapter 7A is limited to new construction in the SRA and the very high fire hazard severity zones in the LRAs.
Individually and collectively, resilient choices in these eight areas reduce the likelihood of ignition that result in catastrophic building loss in a community threatened by wildfire.

While Chapter 7A provides a range of prescriptive and performance-based options, the Suburban Wildfire Adaptation Roadmaps discriminate the fire performance of individual building components based on their characteristics and fill the gap on defensible space guidance.
Flying embers and wind-blown, ground- traveling burning debris are by far the most prevalent attack mechanisms threatening structures during a wildfire.

Buildings can be affected by multiple sources of ignition during a wildfire, including radiative heat transfer from flames, convective heating from wind-blown plumes, continuous or intermittent flame contact, and localized heating from spot fires near the building (Himoto, 2022).
Roof Wind patterns near buildings heavily influence ember distribution patterns and accumulation locations.

Several retrospective studies have reported a statistical correlation between wood roofing and building losses due to ember exposure (Davis, 1990; Foote Insurance Institute for Business & Home Safety 7
In 2020, IBHS released a report on the vulnerability of different roofing materials and roof geometry and updated the report in 2021 (Insurance Institute for Business & Home Safety, 2021).
A Class A roof covering reduces this vulnerability.
Vents Unless a home has been built with an unvented design, unconditioned areas (e.g., attic and crawl spaces) must be ventilated to avoid elevated moisture and temperature conditions.

However, older and inadequately protected vents can provide a pathway for fire and specifically for wind- blown embers to enter the building (Caton et al., 2017; Manzello et al., 2007; Quarles, 2017; Quarles & Gorham, 2019).
Vertical Ground Clearance Wind-blown objects, embers, and debris tend to accumulate at the base of a wall following a change in the wind flow (Manzello et al., 2011).

Full-scale experiments at IBHS (Quarles et al., 2023) and the Fire Research Wind Tunnel Facility in Japan demonstrated that accumulation of embers at the base of an exterior wall can ignite cladding wall is in contact with embers and debris (Manzello et al., 2011; Manzello et al., 2012a; Nazare et al., 2021; Suzuki & Manzello, 2017).
vertical clearance was already required for combustible wood siding by the International Residential Code since 2000 for moisture degradation issues, IBHS began recommending a 6-in.

al (2018) exposed the cladding of a full-scale building to an 80 kW gas burner for 15 min and monitored the temperature on the wall when the test building was oriented at different angles to the wind.
The spread rate is influenced by slope, building size, distance between buildings, and wind direction and intensity (Cohen, 1995; Yuan et al., 2022).
Note that none of the experiments mentioned above incorporate the effects of wind on building-to- building fire spread.

To fill this gap, IBHS committed to studying wind-driven building-to-building fire spread and the first phase of the project—fire spread from approximately 300-sq-ft outbuildings—is currently under investigation.

Photographs of a wind-driven building-to-building fire spread experiment at the IBHS Research Center showing (a) a burning woodshed positioned 30 ft from the main building and (b) a burning metal shed positioned 10 ft from the main building.

Maranghides and Johnsson (2008) reported that flames jetting out of a window ignited a mock wall assembly 6 ft away—the minimum home separation distance required by some building codes—in 80 sec; these tests were performed with no wind.

IBHS post-fire investigations show: (a) fire penetration through combustible cladding and vertical flame spread during the Glass Fire (2020) in Napa and Sonoma Counties, California, likely from a burning vehicle; (b) radiant heat damage from surrounding fires (located more than 50 ft away) damaged the vinyl siding, also during the Glass Fire (2020); (c) T1-11 siding ignited due to fire from surrounding vegetation, likely suppressed by first responders during the Camp Fire (2018) in Butte County, California; (d) fire penetration through combustible cladding during the Waldo Canyon Fire (2012) in El Paso County, Colorado (Maranghides et al., 2015); and (e) in the laboratory, the siding of the building ignited during IBHS’s full-scale wind- driven building-to-building fire spread tests.

Vulnerability of windows: (a) glazing collapsed in Neos Voutzas-Mati, Greece (2018) due to the combustion of a pine tree (Peñaloza et al., 2021), used with permission; (b) windows damaged by radiation from a burning fence about 10 ft away during the Glass Fire (2020) in Napa and Sonoma Counties, California, where the arrow indicates the direction of radiation; (c) the outer tempered pane of a window fell out during an IBHS full-scale test; (d) window damage following the Grass Valley Fire (2006) in San Bernardino County, California, due to a burning building about 40 ft away, documented by Stephen L.

IBHS’s recent findings show that the tempered outer pane of a double-paned window fails after about 10 min of exposure to the heat of a burning shed 30 ft away, and therefore it is necessary to have both panes tempered.
However, wind-blown embers may still accumulate at the base of a door or penetrate small openings around the door and ignite the door jamb.
